era_context: The early-to-mid 14th century, characterized by the decline of the Mongol Yuan Dynasty in China, the consolidation of Ottoman power in Anatolia, the Hundred Years' War in its early phases, and the late medieval period in Europe. This is a time of plague endemic in Asia, political fragmentation, and military innovation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa — virtually no contemporary written sources
Indigenous Americas — no contemporary written records
Eastern Europe and Russia — limited sources

events.title: Plague outbreak reported in Crimea and the Black Sea region
events.description: Plague (bubonic plague, Yersinia pestis) was reported in Crimea and the Black Sea region, with contemporary accounts describing severe mortality. This outbreak is part of the broader plague pandemic spreading westward from Central Asia along trade routes. The disease's presence in Crimea is significant because Genoese and Venetian trading posts in the region would facilitate transmission to the Mediterranean and Europe within the next decade. Islamic and Byzantine sources document this outbreak, which represents a critical stage in the plague's westward progression.
events.certainty_note: Plague in the Black Sea region in the early 1330s is attested in Islamic sources, but exact dating is uncertain. Some sources place plague in Crimea in 1346–1347 rather than 1334. The chronology of plague's westward spread is debated by modern scholars.

events.title: Orhan I consolidates Ottoman power in Anatolia
events.description: Orhan I (Orhan Bey), son of Osman I, continued the consolidation of Ottoman power in northwestern Anatolia. Orhan expanded Ottoman territory through military campaigns against neighboring Turkish beyliks and Byzantine possessions, establishing the Ottoman Empire as a major regional power. He also reformed the military and administrative structures, laying the groundwork for the Ottoman Empire's later expansion into Europe and the Mediterranean. The 1330s saw Orhan's campaigns against Bursa and other key cities, though the exact chronology of individual campaigns is uncertain.
key_figures: Osman I (founder of Ottoman dynasty, deceased)
events.certainty_note: Orhan's reign and campaigns are documented in later Ottoman chronicles and Byzantine sources, but specific dates for individual campaigns in 1334 are uncertain. The broader trajectory of Ottoman expansion is clear, but year-by-year events are difficult to pinpoint.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of plague outbreaks in the Black Sea region is disputed. Some sources place the plague in Crimea in 1346–1347 rather than 1334. Modern scholarship on the plague's westward progression is still debated, with some scholars questioning the exact chronology of transmission from Central Asia to the Mediterranean.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1334 is sparse. No major political or military events are clearly documented for this specific year in most regions. The entries above reflect broader trends (plague progression, Ottoman expansion) rather than discrete events. Islamic and Byzantine sources are the primary documentation for this year, with Chinese sources focusing on court politics rather than specific events. The absence of documentation for most of the world reflects the severe bias of surviving written sources toward the Islamic world, Byzantium, and East Asia.

graph_edges.note: Black Sea plague outbreak preceded European Black Death
Ottoman consolidation led to eventual conquest of Constantinople
